Paul, at Ephesus, found some religious people who looked to Jesus as the Messiah. They had not been led to expect the miraculous powers of the Holy Spirit, nor were they informed that the gospel was especially the ministration of the Spirit. But they said they were ready to welcome it. Paul showed them that John never designed that those he baptized should remain there, but told them that they should believe on Him who would come after him, that is, on Christ Jesus. They thankfully accepted this discovery and were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus. The Holy Spirit came upon them in a surprising, overpowering manner; they spoke with tongues and prophesied, as the apostles and the first Gentile converts did. Though we do not now expect miraculous powers, yet all who profess to be disciples of Christ should be called on to examine whether they have received the seal of the Holy Spirit, in his sanctifying influences, for the sincerity of their faith. Many seem not to have heard that there is a Holy Spirit, and many deem all that is spoken concerning his graces and comforts to be delusion. Of such it may properly be inquired, “To what, then, were you baptized?” for they evidently do not know the meaning of that outward sign on which they place great dependence.

When arguments and persuasions only harden people in disbelief and blasphemy, we must separate ourselves and others from such unholy company. God was pleased to confirm the teaching of these holy men of old, that if their listeners did not believe them, they might believe the works.

It was common, especially among the Jews, for people to profess or try to cast out evil spirits. If we resist the devil by faith in Christ, he will flee from us; but if we think to resist him by using Christ's name or his works as a spell or charm, Satan will prevail against us. Where there is true sorrow for sin, there will be free confession of sin to God in every prayer and to anyone we have offended when the situation requires it. Surely if the word of God prevailed among us, many lewd, infidel, and wicked books would be burned by their owners. Will not these Ephesian converts rise up in judgment against those who profit from such works or allow themselves to possess them? If we desire to be serious about the great work of salvation, every pursuit and enjoyment must be given up which hinders the effect of the gospel upon the mind or loosens its hold upon the heart.

People who traveled from far away to pay their devotions at the temple of Ephesus bought small silver shrines or models of the temple to carry home with them. See how craftsmen take advantage of people's superstition and serve their worldly ends by it. Men are jealous for that by which they get their wealth; and many oppose the gospel of Christ because it calls people from all unlawful crafts, however much wealth is to be gotten by them. There are people who will defend what is most grossly absurd, unreasonable, and false; such as this, that those are gods which are made with hands, if it has worldly interest on its side. The whole city was full of confusion, the common and natural effect of zeal for false religion. Zeal for the honor of Christ and love to the brethren encourage zealous believers to venture into danger. Friends will often be raised up among those who are strangers to true religion but have observed the honest and consistent behavior of Christians.

The Jews came forward in this disturbance. Those who are careful to distinguish themselves from the servants of Christ now, and are afraid of being mistaken for them, will have their judgment accordingly on the great day. One person with authority eventually quieted the noise. It is a very good rule at all times, both in private and public matters, not to be hasty or rash in our actions, but to take time to consider; and always to keep our passions under control. We ought to be calm and do nothing rashly; do nothing in haste that we may regret later. The regular methods of the law should always stop popular disturbances, and they will do so in well-governed nations. Most people are more concerned about what others think than about God’s judgment. How good it would be if we would quiet our disorderly desires and passions by considering the account we must soon give to the Judge of heaven and earth! And see how God's providence keeps the public peace, by an inexplicable power over people’s spirits. Thus the world is kept in some order, and men are held back from harming each other. We can scarcely look around but we see people act like Demetrius and the workmen. It is as safe to contend with wild animals as with men enraged by party zeal and disappointed greed, who think that all arguments are settled when they have shown that they become rich through practices that are opposed. Whatever side in religious disputes, or whatever name this spirit assumes, it is worldly, and should be discouraged by all who value truth and piety. And let us not be disheartened; the Lord on high is more powerful than the noise of many waters; he can still the anger of the people.
